data.content_ref: inline:The mechanism relies on 'mutual knowledge of finite copper/tin reserves' driving defection and arms races, but the scope explicitly excludes 'Climate as exogenous forcing.' This creates an internal tension: if copper and tin are finite, their scarcity must have a cause—depletion, supply-chain disruption, or something else. The account offers no endogenous reason for why reserves became binding precisely around 1200 BC after centuries of stable trade. Without an exogenous trigger (climate stress, mining exhaustion, or similar), the sudden shift from cooperative equilibrium to defection is unexplained; the game-theoretic structure describes how collapse propagated once triggered but not why the trigger occurred when it did. Excluding climate is legitimate, but the account must then supply an alternative trigger within its own terms, which it does not.
